sast_false_positives.false_positive_reasoning: The scanner flagged a concatenation (template["title"] + suffix) at line 145, but that concatenated value is passed as a bound parameter to asyncpg, not interpolated into the SQL text. The SQL statement on lines 138–143 uses positional placeholders ($1..$8) and the subsequent arguments (lines 144–151) are provided separately to conn.execute; asyncpg will bind them as parameters, preventing SQL injection. Other DB calls are similarly parameterized (e.g. seed_experiment uses $1::jsonb at lines 176–186 and passes json.dumps at 187–191) or are static SQL (seed_test_tenant SELECT on line 169, refresh_m1_dashboard call on line 215). The only string concatenation is done in Python (line 145) using internal data (SAMPLE_JOBS and a loop-derived suffix), not by injecting into the SQL text, so there is no practical attack path in this code as presented.

sast_false_positives.false_positive_reasoning: Language/framework: Python using asyncpg (Postgres). The reported string-concatenation SQL injection is a false positive because all database statements in this file use asyncpg positional parameters ($1, $2, $3) rather than string interpolation. For example: the 90-day scan uses a parameterized fetch (lines 28-40) where d90 is passed as $1 (line 40); inserts/updates use $1/$2/$3 placeholders (INSERT at lines 44-49; UPDATE at lines 114-119; fetchrow at lines 147-153; UPDATEs at lines 190-195 and 200-203). The d90 value on line 25 is computed server-side (datetime arithmetic) and is not user-controlled. Even tenant_id (function param in auto_create_renewal_invoice) is bound as a parameter ($1) instead of being concatenated into SQL, so an attacker cannot inject SQL via those paths. No SQL statements in this file perform non-literal string concatenation to build query text, so there is no practical attack vector here.

sast_false_positives.false_positive_reasoning: The reported code does not construct SQL by concatenating user input into the query text; it uses asyncpg positional parameters ($1..$4). In get_audit_log (lines 52–62) the SQL text contains placeholders (WHERE tenant_id = $1 AND action LIKE $4 LIMIT $2 OFFSET $3) and the values (tenant_id, limit, offset, f"%{action_filter}%") are passed as bound parameters to conn.fetch. The f-string at line 61 only builds the LIKE pattern in Python and that entire pattern is supplied as a single parameter — it cannot change SQL syntax or inject clauses. Similarly, record_audit_event (lines 32–40) and export_audit_log_csv (lines 95–103) use $ placeholders with values passed separately (json.dumps(details) and str(days)), so user input never alters the SQL statement itself. Because asyncpg binds parameters server-side, there is no practical SQL injection attack vector in these snippets.

sast_false_positives.false_positive_reasoning: The report flags string concatenation into an SQL statement around line 106, but the code is using asyncpg positional parameters, so there is no SQL text built from user input. Specifically: the INSERT at lines 106–114 uses a literal SQL string with $1, $2, $3 placeholders and passes tenant_id, "Personal", and slug as bound parameters (lines 111–114). The slug is constructed via an f-string at line 104 that includes user_id, but that computed value is passed as a parameter (line 113) rather than interpolated into the SQL text — asyncpg binds parameters safely and prevents injection. Earlier queries (lines 71–81 and 85–92) also use $1/$2 placeholders. Therefore there is no practical SQL injection attack path in this code. If the intent was to warn about using user_id in a slug (line 104), that is a potential application-level concern (e.g., predictable or cosmetic data), but it is not an SQL injection risk given the parameterized usage shown.

sast_false_positives.false_positive_reasoning: This is a false positive: the code constructs a parameterized query and does not directly interpolate user input into the SQL. Lines 92–120 build the query using $N placeholders (e.g. f" AND category = ${idx}" and f" AND (name ILIKE ${idx} OR description ILIKE ${idx})") and append actual values into params; the call at line 122 uses conn.fetch(base, *params) so asyncpg sends values as parameters (safe against SQL injection). The only string concatenation of a non-literal is the ORDER BY at lines 106–113 (base += f" ORDER BY {order}"), but 'order' is chosen from a hard-coded whitelist/dict based on the incoming sort value (line 106) and falls back to the static default. Since ORDER BY cannot be parameterized, the whitelist approach is the correct mitigant — no user-supplied string is directly embedded. In short: placeholders + parameter binding (lines 114–119 and 122) prevent injection; the dynamic ORDER BY is constrained to static, known values, so there is no practical attack vector in the shown code. (If the whitelist were removed or expanded to include raw user input, injection risk would become real.)

sast_false_positives.false_positive_reasoning: The reported issue is a false positive. The only non-literal SQL construction is in list_jobs (lines 316–337) where the code appends SQL fragments like f" AND location ILIKE ${n}" and f" AND (title ILIKE ${n} OR ...)". The f-strings only inject the parameter index (n), which is an integer controlled by the server-side code flow (lines 322–335), not by user input. Actual user-supplied values (location, min_salary, keywords) are appended to the params list (lines 327, 331, 335) and passed separately to asyncpg via conn.fetch(query, *params) (line 337). asyncpg uses positional parameters ($1, $2, ...) so user data is bound as parameters, not interpolated into the SQL string — preventing SQL injection. Other queries in the file consistently use parameter placeholders (e.g., lines 72–84, 149–164, 241–252), further indicating correct parameterization. In summary, although the SQL string is assembled dynamically for placeholder numbering, no user-controlled input is injected into the SQL text itself, so there is no practical SQL injection attack path in this code.
